product_target.statement: Product identity and current-deliverable authority are contested across the seed bundle: sources describe Moria as a reusable GPU-resident voxel-world substrate (Rust crate/family) whose walkable-world executable is only a public-API validation harness, while Product One frames a near-term product-shaped walkable-world demo (generated region, third-person character, dig/place proof) as the milestone deliverable. All sources agree the full game, System/LLM, spells, gas, combat, AI, and building layers are out of scope for this repository. Which framing binds product identity and current scope is unresolved without human authority resolution.

documents.notes: Top-level product blurb aligning Moria with a reusable substrate and a separate validation harness. Descriptive only; does not itself establish binding authority over more detailed seeds.

Seed-bundle index that labels other documents as binding or reference. Those labels are evidence to analyze, not self-authenticating authority. Treat as an index/ordering aid, not a product-target definition.

Presents an operator clarification that Moria's product is the reusable substrate crate(s) and that the walkable-world executable is only a public-API harness. Contends for binding product-identity and consumer-boundary authority; not confirmed over Product One without human resolution.

Defines Product One ('The Walkable World'): substrate slice, curated seed region, third-person controller, dig/place proof, performance targets, and ordered milestones. Contends for binding current-deliverable authority; relationship to project-boundary product identity is unresolved.

Architecture/design specification for the full voxel-world substrate vision. Seeds index and Product One treat it as a companion reference from which the milestone selects a subset; broader layers (CA, integrity, building, System hooks, full fluids) exceed Product One non-goals. Companion pivot doc is missing.

claims.statement: Moria is a reusable, GPU-resident voxel-world substrate intended to be consumed by the actual game as a Rust crate.
Its walkable-world executable is a separate consumer and validation harness for terrain generation, streaming, meshing, editing, collision, persistence, and performance—not a game layer.
product-one-seed.md defines the binding substrate implementation and its walkable-world validation harness.
voxel-world-substrate.md is the substrate architecture reference. Only the portions selected by the Product One seed are required for this milestone.
project-boundary.md records the operator's binding clarification: Moria is a reusable crate consumed by a separate game, and the included executable is only a public-API validation harness.
Moria is only the voxel-world substrate. Broader game, System, LLM, spell, gas, combat, AI, and building intent is deliberately absent and out of scope.
Moria's product is the reusable voxel-world substrate, exposed as a Rust crate or a small family of tightly scoped Rust crates.
The actual game is a separate downstream consumer and is not part of this repository.

Moria may include a walkable-world executable, but that executable is only a validation harness. It must consume the substrate through the same public interfaces available to an external game rather than owning privileged or game-specific implementation paths.

This is an immediate, concrete reason to use a Cargo workspace boundary between the reusable substrate and its validation harness. The precise crate split is a technical-design decision; the consumer boundary is not optional.

Game rules and the future System, LLM, spell, gas, combat, AI, and building layers are out of scope. Compatibility seams may be designed where the substrate requirements demand them, but those layers must not be implemented here.
Scope: the voxel substrate, one generated region, and a character who can run around in it. No System, no spells, no gas, no game. This is the tech proven as a product-shaped demo.
Everything here references voxel-world-substrate.md; this doc pins down what gets built first, what the seed world contains, and what "done" looks like.

A generated natural world — hills, forest, river, cliffs, caves — that you can run through in third person, where everything you see is voxel truth: you can walk to any point, the terrain is continuous and smooth, and a debug key proves it's all mutable matter underneath. The demo's job is to make one claim undeniable: this is not a heightmap with props — it's a fully material world, and it looks good.

Non-goals (explicitly out): Combat, stats, entities beyond the player, AI of any kind; The System / LLM anything; Gas, pricing, intent; Building UI, blueprints, mechanisms; Fluids beyond static bodies (tier 1 only); Weather sim, seasons, growth; Persistence beyond reload the same seed + deltas (single save slot, no versioning).
Dig and place stay in scope, on a debug key. Not as gameplay — as the proof.
Region: 1km × 1km × 256m (surface at ~64m); 25cm voxels, 16³ bricks per the substrate doc. Voxel-size final call happens here — this region is the benchmark bed for the 25cm vs 12.5cm question.

Terrain composition the generator must produce: rolling meadow with grass dressing; dense mixed forest (2 tree species + bushes); river with carved channel + a lake; cliff face / rocky outcrop with exposed strata; one karst cave system walkable from surface mouth to ~-40m; one aquifer band and one ore vein type crossing the cave; boulders, stumps, scattered rocks; a small ruin (hand-stamped blueprint, placed by POI metadata).

Materials palette (seed set, ~14): air, water, topsoil, subsoil, sand, gravel, limestone, sandstone, shale, granite, iron-ore, wood, leaf, cut-stone (ruin). State nibble exists in the format but no CA consumes it in product one.

Product one builds generation layer full (continent pass may be stubbed); matter layer partial (brick pool, homogeneous sentinels, lazy materialization, meshing, grass/clutter, voxel-object placement/render yes; felling/rigid conversion no; CA/fire/fluids-tier-2/3/integrity/granular settle no; static water tier 1 yes); script/API layer sliver (dig/place verbs and mirror queries as engine-internal API; no embedded scripting language yet).

Third-person character controller: run, sprint, jump, swim (surface paddle), collision against voxel truth (capsule vs. brick occupancy, not vs. render mesh); free-orbit third-person camera; traversal must exercise Z including walkable cave route; debug palette for dig/place sphere, wireframe/brick-boundary, raw-voxel toggle, streaming-ring visualizer, time-of-day slider.

Performance targets: 60fps at 1440p on mid GPU (3060-class) and 60fps at 1080p–1440p on M4 Mac Mini; dig-to-remesh within 2 frames with no hitch on 3m-radius carve; cold-start < 5s to walkable; full region under ~2GB GPU resident with streaming rings; delta save < 50MB after heavy defacement with exact restore. Benchmarks (scripted flythrough + carve-storm plus machine profile) are part of the deliverable.

Dev-platform constraints (M4 Mac Mini, 32GB unified, wgpu/Metal): no 64-bit buffer atomics; bandwidth is the ceiling; keep FleX command/mirror architecture; stay on wgpu/WGSL with no native Metal fork in load-bearing layers; discrete-GPU targets provisional until Linux box returns.

Ordered demoable milestones: (1) hill that looks like a hill; (2) carve a smooth tunnel; (3) true geology; (4) dressed world; (5) the run (controller + continuous cliff-to-cave); (6) numbers (streaming, persistence, benchmarks); stretch (7) timber/felled tree.

What Product One buys: substrate crates exist, benchmarked, with API boundary enforced; public artifact for X audience with downloadable demo at milestone 5; decision bed for open substrate questions answered with measurements; Product two starts from a walkable world instead of a whiteboard.
This substrate must stand alone as an engine layer with zero LLM dependency. The System (LLM) hooks are noted where they attach but are not required for the substrate.

Design goals: reads as a normal world; mutable everywhere all the way down; deep Z first-class; substrate not game (same crate stack for ARPG, DF-style fortress, Moria-style descent, or pure sandbox); GPU-resident brick pool / CA / FleX-pattern coupling per the pivot doc.

Hybrid look strategy: terrain and structures use smooth isosurface extraction (surface nets or dual contouring); vegetation and clutter are voxel-backed objects or instanced dressing, not terrain cubes. Voxel size assumed 25cm; 16³ bricks.

Storage: 16³ brick pool with homogeneous sentinels; per-voxel material/density/state payload; per-brick aggregates; 2D column index for surface height, Z-slice views, and lazy materialization from columns.

World generation is geology-first: continent/climate, terrain, strata, cave/void passes as pure functions over coordinates + seed; lazy brick materialization on first touch; System attaches only via metadata/placement, not geology generation.

Vegetation rule: everything that can burn, break, or block is voxel-backed; else dressing anchored to voxels. Trees and micro props are voxel objects; grass/clutter is instanced dressing from scatter points; granular materials get settle CA.
Fluids use three tiers: static bodies (lakes/seas), coarse brick-aggregate flow, and fine PBD splash particles.

Layering: game layer above semantic layer above script/API layer above matter layer above generation layer. Gas is a policy object at script/API; System is a game-layer client; nothing above the matter layer touches voxels directly.
claims.locator: ## 12. Layering: The Substrate as Reusable Crates

claims.statement: Substrate build order includes brick pool, meshing, dig/place, geology, brick CA (fire/wetness) + tree felling, fluids tier 1+2, integrity + granular, persistence + streaming — a broader vertical slice than Product One selects.

Open questions: voxel size 25cm vs 12.5cm (possibly per-region); chunked marching-cubes LOD vs imposter cards; object-layer capacity / spatial acceleration; fluids tier 2 pressure solve vs momentum-only; multiplayer scope statements.
Document is written as companion to system-substrate-pivot.md and builds on GPU-resident / FleX pivot decisions without relitigating them; that companion file is not present in the repository.

Architecture doc also specifies weather/seasons/fire ecology, structural integrity and cave-ins, building placement/blueprints/mechanisms/rooms, entities/pathfinding/Z-levels, and full persistence/streaming ring semantics for fortress and ARPG modes — capabilities Product One marks out of scope or stretch.

decisions.question: What is the authoritative product identity for this repository (Moria)?

options.label: Reusable voxel-world substrate crate(s)
options.summary: Moria's product is the reusable GPU-resident substrate exposed as Rust crate(s). Any walkable-world binary is only a validation harness and public-API consumer, not the product itself.

options.label: Product One walkable-world demo
options.summary: The near-term product is the product-shaped walkable-world demo (generated region, third-person character, dig/place proof, downloadable milestone artifact), with the substrate existing to serve that demo.

options.label: Substrate product with Product One as first milestone
options.summary: Long-term product identity is the reusable substrate; Product One is the first demoable milestone and harness scope that proves substrate crates, without redefining the product as a game.

decisions.rationale: Root README and project-boundary state the product is the reusable substrate crate with a non-game harness. Product One is titled as a product seed for 'The Walkable World' and describes a product-shaped demo with player controller, trailer shots, and a downloadable demo. Seeds README asserts both product-one and project-boundary as binding. No human feedback selects among these framings; evidence alone is not permission to choose.

decisions.question: Which seed documents are binding for product identity and current deliverable scope?

options.label: project-boundary binds identity; product-one binds milestone scope
options.summary: Treat project-boundary as binding product-identity/consumer-boundary constraints; product-one as binding current implementation milestone; voxel-world-substrate as supporting architecture reference only for selected slices.

options.label: product-one is sole binding target
options.summary: Treat product-one-seed as the binding product/deliverable definition; project-boundary and root README as supporting context; voxel-world-substrate as non-binding architecture reference.

options.label: project-boundary sole binding; product-one non-binding demo brief
options.summary: Treat only project-boundary (operator clarification) as binding; demote product-one to supporting milestone notes that do not expand product identity beyond substrate-plus-harness.

options.label: Accept seeds README role labels as written
options.summary: Accept the index labels: product-one binding for implementation/harness, project-boundary binding clarification, voxel-world-substrate architecture reference with Product One selection.

decisions.rationale: Seeds README uses the word 'binding' for both product-one and project-boundary, but filename, ordering, and in-document labels are not self-authenticating. Project-boundary and product-one can be read as complementary or as competing primary authorities. No human feedback assigns document roles.

decisions.question: What is in scope for the current deliverable?

options.label: Full Product One seed (milestones 1–6)
options.summary: Current deliverable is the Product One walkable world: curated 1km region, substrate slice as specified, third-person controller and camera, dig/place proof, performance targets, and ordered milestones through benchmarks. Architecture content beyond Product One selection is out of milestone scope.

options.label: Substrate crates and API only
options.summary: Current deliverable is reusable substrate crates with public mutation/query APIs and optional minimal harness; character controller, postcard demo route, and downloadable playable demo are not required product deliverables.

options.label: Substrate plus non-game validation harness
options.summary: Deliver substrate crates plus a walkable-world executable that validates generation, streaming, meshing, editing, collision, persistence, and performance through public APIs, without treating the harness as a product-shaped game demo or X-downloadable game artifact.

options.label: Full voxel-world-substrate build order
options.summary: Current deliverable tracks the architecture doc build order including CA, fluids tier 2, integrity, granular settle, and related systems—not only the Product One subset.

decisions.rationale: Product One specifies a detailed milestone deliverable including player controller and demo narrative. Project-boundary frames the executable strictly as a validation harness and excludes game layers. The architecture reference describes a wider substrate build order than Product One includes. Seeds README says only Product One-selected portions of the architecture are required for this milestone. These statements define mutually exclusive current-scope options; without human resolution none is selected.

decisions.question: What is the authoritative role of the walkable-world executable relative to the substrate?

options.label: Public-API validation harness only
options.summary: Executable exists solely to validate substrate public APIs and performance; must not own privileged paths; not a game layer or primary product artifact.

options.label: Primary product-shaped demo artifact
options.summary: Executable is the Product One deliverable for audience demos and downloadable milestone 5; substrate crates are supporting infrastructure for that demo.

options.label: Harness that is also the Product One demo
options.summary: Same binary serves as API-faithful validation harness and as the product-shaped walkable demo described in Product One, without becoming a game layer.

decisions.rationale: Sources agree the walkable world is not the full game, but disagree on whether it is merely a harness, the product-shaped demo, or both. This affects deliverable definition and packaging. No human feedback resolves it.

deferred_design_questions.question: Final world-grid voxel size: 25cm vs 12.5cm (or per-region mixed resolution)?

options.label: Per-region mixed (fine near settlements, coarse wilderness)

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Product One assumes 25cm and nominates the seed region as the benchmark bed; architecture lists the tradeoff as an open question. Measurement-driven technical choice, not product-identity authority.

deferred_design_questions.question: What is the precise Cargo crate split within the substrate workspace?

options.label: Single substrate crate plus harness package
Family of crates aligned to generation/matter/script layers
Defer split until module boundaries emerge from implementation

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Project-boundary requires a workspace consumer boundary but explicitly leaves precise crate split as technical design.

deferred_design_questions.question: Primary terrain meshing algorithm: surface nets vs dual contouring (and hard-face crystalline materials)?

options.label: Dual contouring with per-material crystalline hard faces

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Architecture recommends smooth extraction with dual contouring for sharp cuts; Product One requires full meshing without locking the algorithm. Implementation choice.

deferred_design_questions.question: Distant terrain LOD: chunked marching-cubes LOD vs imposter cards from the column index?
deferred_design_questions.rationale: Listed as an open architecture question; does not change product identity or whether Product One is the deliverable.

deferred_design_questions.question: At what tree/object count does the object registry need its own spatial acceleration structure?

options.label: No dedicated acceleration for Product One counts
Add spatial acceleration when profiling demands

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Scaling/perf design question for the object layer; not an authority conflict.

deferred_design_questions.question: If/when fluids tier 2 is built, does it need a pressure solve or is momentum-only enough?
deferred_design_questions.rationale: Product One excludes fluids beyond static tier-1 bodies. Solver choice is future architecture, not current product authority.

deferred_design_questions.question: Should multiplayer/server-authoritative readiness remain in scope statements even if not built?

options.label: Keep server-authoritative verb architecture in scope language
Omit multiplayer from scope until a dedicated plan

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Architecture notes multiplayer readiness by construction; Product One does not require multiplayer. Wording/design hygiene, not product-target selection.

deferred_design_questions.question: Is tree felling with rigid-body fall in the current milestone or only stretch/future?

options.label: Out of scope entirely for this product line

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Product One marks felling as stretch; architecture treats falling trees as non-negotiable for later game fantasies. Implementation prioritization once deliverable authority is settled—not itself a document-authority conflict.

deferred_design_questions.question: How should GPU-resident / FleX / particle-layer decisions that were deferred to system-substrate-pivot.md be recovered?

options.label: Rederive pivot constraints from in-repo references only
Supply the missing pivot document before detailed GPU design
Defer low-level GPU kernel design until pivot material exists

deferred_design_questions.rationale: Missing companion is a provenance gap for architecture details (FleX coupling, dynamic matter/particles). Product identity and Product One non-goals remain readable without it; recovery strategy is design process, not product-target authority. Recorded in missing_references.
